narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, predicting whether they'd pass on the first try based on academic and personal factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 34 (under the usual 36.7), Year 1 decile rank of 3rd out of 10 (versus an average 6th), Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10 (well below the average 6th), and plans for full-time work—and estimated a 40.5% chance you'd fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability crossed the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward expecting you'd pass despite other concerns. However, your Year 3 decile rank in school played a major counteracting role, significantly pushing the model toward predicting failure because it was so much lower than what's typical for successful bar takers. Your middle family income bracket also helped somewhat, nudging things slightly in favor of passing compared to lower-income peers. Overall, while your LSAT showed real promise, the low Year 3 performance stood out as the biggest red flag when stacked against averages, tipping the balance. Full-time status, common for 93% of students, didn't sway things much either way. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements—but the model saw risks based purely on these patterns from past students. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, aim to retake the LSAT for a score closer to or above 37, and consider part-time options if they align with building a stronger record. Gathering letters of explanation for your GPA dips or seeking mentorship from bar-passers in your community could also strengthen future apps. With deliberate steps, you can shift these predictions in your favor for the next cycle.
